Elite attire in the Mongol Empire: 

The aristocratic deel from the 13th-14th cent. cemetery at Bukhyn Khoshuu in Delgerkhaan County, Khentii Province, Mongolia. 

1/n

#ClassicsTober23: Persephone. 

Every archaeological museum in Sicily is heaving with statues and other representations of Demeter and her daughter, Persephone. It shows how much corn growing meant to them. 

This polychrome example is from Syracuse &  is quite delightful

📷mine  🏺 ClassicsBlueSky

New OA paper by my team (lead: PhD student Silvia Gazzo) presenting taphonomic & archeological data to explain the presence of microshells (specifically Bittium) in Paleolithic sites, with Riparo Bombrini (Liguria, Italy) as a case study. We confirm their introduction was prob unintentional BUT... 🧪

I love the depth of his fresco. It feels like you’re actually standing in an orchard and could pluck one of those succulents fruits and gobble it up. It’s from the villa of Livia, Augustus’ wife, and is just one of many beautiful frescoes found there. 

🏛️📷 Palazzo Massimo, Rome
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Especially for @@42425, an ancient arch inside the church of St Andrew's, Corbridge. The building is mostly medieval, with a bit of Saxon, but this huge arch is all Roman. It was lifted from the ruins of the Roman town nearby and rebuilt here, a nice example of early recycling.

#TombTuesday - The Lion Tomb in the Qasr al-Bint Nabatean Necropolis of Hegra (now in Saudi Arabia). The tomb is decorated with a carving of two felines on either side of a rosette. The name of the stonemason who carved the tomb, Aftah, is engraved above the left feline.

The Crosby Garrett Helmet is an extremely fine Roman cavalry helmet dating from the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD. It consists of a face mask and a headpiece in the shape of a Phrygian cap topped by a griffin. It was discovered by a metal detectorist in Cumbria (UK) in 2010.

A selection of Roman glass vessels excavated from sites on the island of Malta; many of then would have been used to hold perfume or scented oil. They're quite simple in design, but still very striking, nearly 2000 years after they were originally made!

I love this book. Though written in the 70s, its scholarship holds up. Horse collars, invented in China, reached Europe by the 900s and revolutionized agriculture.

The very first picture *anywhere* of a horse pulling a plough comes from the 11th C Bayeux Tapestry!

#medievalsky

The earliest known manufactured mirrors (approximately 8000 years old), found at the Neolithic site of Çatalhöyük in Turkey. These mirrors were made from obsidian (volcanic glass), and had a convex surface and remarkably good optical quality.

Fledgling California condors at Marble Canyon in northern Arizona. 
722 (right) was photographed in December 2013, and 1150 (left) in March 2023.
Both hatched from eggs laid in the wild.

A Blue-winged Mountain Tanager enjoys some fresh fruit. These birds are common and widespread in the Andean cloud forest. I believe the plant is a Clusia sp. Birds provide an important service to the forest by spreading fruit seeds. 
🧪🌿🪶
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

My fav gull! 90% of them nest on Isla Rasa in the Gulf of California but a small population began nesting on an island in a small lake and then on the roof of a McDonalds in Seaside, CA near Monterey. The island eroded & McD's burned down, but some friends of mine built a new island for them!

The Velvet-purple Coronet is a fabulous hummingbird that looks velvety black in low light, but catch it at the right angle and their feathers shine purple! They are found in the cloud forests of Ecuador and Colombia.

🧪The Andean cock-of-the-rock, a clear example of sexual dimorphism! Males have a large crest and vibrant orange coloration, while females are more subdued in their brown plumage. Interestingly, even their iris color is sexually dimorphic, with only females exhibiting that striking blue 👀

4 bald eagles (1 adult & 3 juvies, perhaps the triplets born last year on the lake) trying to hunt a cormorant or kleptoparasitize it, aka harassing it until it gives up fish that it caught - a strategy often used by eagles since they are big enough to bully other animals to steal food! #SciComm 🧪🦑🦈

New bowerbird paper! Male great bowerbirds build display courts that are linked to mating success, and we found that object availability 🧱 rather than male building ability 👷 explains variation in court quality rb.gy/vznmm7

“The left side of its body was covered in shimmering spring-green feathers, the classic coloring for females. Its right side, however, was iridescent blue, the telltale marker of a male. The bird appeared to be a bilateral gynandromorph: female on one side and male on the other..."

(gift link)

Ridgway’s rail! 🪶

This endangered species is easily heard in parts of the Bay Area, but very hard to see as they skulk in marsh grass. At Arrowhead Marsh, I’ve seen one just twice in 15 trips—fleetingly, at a distance. 

But yesterday, because of the very high tide, long, clear sightings of FOUR.
My favourite photography subject at the moment is the kuaka (bar-tailed godwit). It’s about this time of year that they set off on the longest migration in the world - from Aotearoa to Alaska. 🪶

Endangered California gnatcatcher in the wonderful coastal sage habitat here down by the Mexican border in the Tijuana River Valley open space preserve. In the first shot he is doing his mewing call that sounds like a kitten. 🪶

Female Red-winged Blackbirds are acrobatic insect hunters! 

A few years ago I happened upon one dancing on pond muck - I was entranced. Now I go look for them and feel joy at seeing and hearing their little contact calls to fledglings on the shore. 🪶🌿
A white-tailed ptarmigan along a trail up La Plata Peak, CO. The smallest grouse, it stays at in the alpine/subalpine year round, mainly eating willows. It's been recorded nesting at over 13,000 ft 🪶

Two nēnēs at WWT Slimbridge! Native to Hawai'i, and the rarest goose in the world, at one point numbering only 30 birds due to hunting and introduced predators like mongoose, pigs, and cats. Decades of conservation efforts have helped reintroduce the nēnē and native numbers are over 3,800 today 😊🪶
Northern Fulmars aren't normally a beach birds but windrows of copepods were making for an easy meal so large rafts of fulmars were following the banquet. Last June along Jan Mayen, north Atlantic 🪶🦑

3rd plumage white-tailed eagle.

The white-tailed eagle (Haliaeetus albicilla), known for its distinctive white tail in adulthood, is one of the largest birds of prey in Eurasia, thriving near large bodies of water.

Photo taken in Lohja, Finland, February 2024.

A Torrent Tyrannulet (Serpophaga cinerea) delivering some snacks to the kids. These tiny flycatchers (about 10 cm or 4 in) live on fast moving rivers and streams and catch insects right at the edge of the water. I found this nesting pair in March of this year. #birds 🪶#costarica

A park ranger in Santee SP in SC once pointed out to me that the round nesting holes in the trees in the middle of Lake Marion are from Pileated Woodpeckers, but the large oval ones were made decades ago by Ivory Billed Woodpeckers, and this fact honestly haunts me - all that's left is a void. 🪶

I've seen 72 species in Texas. 68 of them were before breakfast today at Audubon's Mitchell Lake preserve. What a place! Black-bellied whistling duck, Couch's kingbird, Bewick's wren, loggerhead shrike, ladderback woodpecker, white-faced ibis & vesper sparrow were all lifers.🪶

kirtland’s warblers are the rarest breeding warbler in the united states. they’re also tail-bobbing chonks with no fear of humans and a peculiar love of fire. this one has a pink mandible which means it was born just a few weeks ago!

Swans in Europe, Asia, and the Americas are white. For centuries in Europe, there were idioms like "might as well look for a black swan" which was assumed to be impossible.

But all along, Australia was full of black swans 🪶 They're pretty easy to find here.
Minnippi Park, Brisbane
#ausbirds

This spring, when doing partial garden tidying, I left the dead swamp milkweed stems tall because birds liked to perch there. I'm so glad I did; I discovered that female Baltimore orioles carefully work the fibers out of them, creating beautiful long string for weaving their nests. Not only that, +

A Little Blue Heron and some White Ibises doing some afternoon fishing.

Ibis Fact: White Ibis nests can have nests from ground to as high up as 50ft 

L.B. Heron Fact: These Herons have white feathers that transition to its blue color over the first year of their lives/

#FloridaWildlife #Birds

Fall migration had started here in Minnesota, bringing with it nighthawks in need of help. Living most of their lives on the wing, nighthawks even drink while flying, swooping down over water to grab a sip. It makes them very difficult patients since they don't readily eat or drink without aid.

Cassin’s Finch brings #BirdGlamour to conifer & quaking aspen forests in western North America! Similar to the House Finch but with a pointier crown, Cassin’s Finches are excellent mimics and add other species’ songs to theirs. They’re also a bit salty: they love salt and visit mineral deposits. 🐡🧪🐦

We were happy to find a pair of Powerful Woodpeckers in Ecuador, where they aren't very common. If you wonder what an Ivory-billed Woodpecker looked like in the wild, the Powerful Woodpecker is in the same genus and looks similar.
Female (L), Male (R) 
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

American flamingos are one of the largest species of flamingo. Found in the Caribbean and along the northern coast of South America, these extraordinarily social birds live in flocks that sometimes number in the thousands. A pigment in their food gives their feathers their flamboyant pink color.

Here’s some travel inspo for Bird of the Week: The Blackpoll Warbler has the longest migration of any North American warbler. Each fall, most Blackpolls migrate over 2,000 miles across without stopping, sometimes flying over 80 hours straight! Learn more at 
bit.ly/3ReK8Lb. 🪶

Prob the best studied climate-related range expansion of any European #birds is the Cetti’s Warbler. This skulking brown bird is the sole European representative of a largely Asian genus & is a resident species of reedbeds & wet scrub. 1/N 🌿🌏 🧪

Pics Nick Goodram CC BY 2.0 flickr.com/search/?user...

Red-capped Cardinals can be found broadly throughout the Amazon river basin. They belong to a genus of tanagers, not cardinals, because apparently we like common names for birds to be confusing. 🪶 🧪
Cuyabeno Reserve, Ecuador 2022

Best bird photos from 2023.
When word got out that rare scarlet-chested parrots had been seen in big numbers at Gluepot, birders (including me) flocked to see them. I had searched for these birds for years. This is a female scarlet-chested parrot feeding on cottony saltbush.
🪶 #birds

The black-headed gull is a small gull that breeds in much of the Palearctic including Europe and also in coastal eastern Canada. Most of the population is migratory and winters further south, but some birds reside in the milder westernmost areas of Europe

The flame bowerbird is one of the most brilliantly colored bowerbirds. The male is a medium-sized bird, up to 25 cm long, with flame orange and golden yellow plumage, elongated neck plumes, and a yellow-tipped black tail. It builds an "avenue-type" bower with two side walls of sticks.

Though I know it’s the most colorful, flashy birds that usually draw us into birding, I’ve come to be more interested in the subtleties that distinguish diverse but sometimes overlooked groups, like sparrows. Here, though soon to be renamed, a juvenile Harris’ Sparrow in Bixby, OK USA. 🪶

More good news! We've found a record number of the scarce Brown Hairstreak #butterfly (Thecla betulae) eggs at Rock Edge SSSI. Forty nine eggs around the site, including 2 triplets and 2 doubles. Only 3 eggs on Blackthorn, 36 on Cherry Plum and 10 on another Prunus species...
#UKWildlife #OX3

This handsome little scooter is a Cinnamon Flycatcher. They call the Andean cloud forest home and can be found from Venezuela south to Chile. Like most flycatchers you can see them sallying from a perch to catch flying insects. Montezuma Rainforest Lodge, Colombia 🪶🧪🌿

🪶🦉Rose-ringed parakeet, enjoying some winter sunshine in an Antwerp park, where they established a feral colony in the last decade. They seem to perform assembly flights above the city at dusk, before roosting together. 
(halsbandparkiet, Psittacula krameri)

Hopalong is back! My one legged blackbird, not seen for months, reappeared in the garden this morning in his usual eating place, no longer molting, a full array of feathers. Now fat and full in his usual perch. Definitely him, same markings on his tail and by his left eye. This makes me so happy!

Pink cockatoo
The pink cockatoo, also known as Major Mitchell's cockatoo or Leadbeater's cockatoo, is a medium-sized cockatoo that inhabits arid and semi-arid inland areas across Australia, except the northeast.

A Grey Catbird I saw at MacGregor Point PP in Ontario.

Catbirds like to skulk around in the bushes so it was a treat to see one in the relative open. They are related to thrashers and mockingbirds, so as well as their namesake mewing, they have a vast repertoire of elaborate vocalizations.

Down on the flood plain in search of a rare visitor to these lakes. A morning of mud-splatter and cracked ice; of snipe flashing off the wet meadows and grey partridges thundering from thickets. And then there it was, the sudden immensity of a white-tailed sea eagle watching the winter waters.

This is Cassin's sparrow--a bird of the SW grasslands that, until yesterday, had never been recorded in the East Bay before. It was spotted at Albany Bulb in the am & several dozen happy birders went to see it. I LOVE how much communal joy a little brown bird (w/ a stunning voice) can generate. 🪶📷

I got to see this very photogenic lifer yesterday: a brilliant white Lark Sparrow that was hanging out in a flock of finches and bluebirds, foraging among the dew-covered grass at a local campground. 🪶

Frigatebirds have tiny little legs and feet. They can't land on water because they are unable to run for a takeoff roll like waterfowl. So what is a thirsty, dirty frigatebird to do? Crash and fly, my friends. Amazing to see this.
Floreana Island, Galapagos, 2018

Birding level reached: 🪶 Resplendent Quetzal 
This bird, a member of the trogon family, is one of the most extravagant natural users of metamaterials. Backlit, its feathers appear a dull gray, but when light reflects, they light up, traversing blue-green-golden depending on the angle. #wildlife
One thing I always love  about visiting the American tropics is seeing Trogons. Here's a Black-headed we saw at the Belize Zoo (Which is less a zoo than a wildlife rescue and sanctuary) #birding

Broad-billed motmot with a quite water-soaked feather dress.

Bet he was as surprised as me when the sudden rain shower started 🌧️

Spotting it was pure luck: Motmots usually wait motionless on branches until potential prey moves (insects).

🪶
#birds
#costarica

Burrowing owls (Athene cunicularia) are found throughout the Americas wherever vegetation is low, and soil permits burrows.

They're prevalent in the mountain states of Colorado, Texas and New Mexico, especially in area where prairie dogs create convenient burrows.

(pic: Wendy Miller)

When I see a hawk perching, it's usually very far away and 80 to 100 feet up.  This red-shouldered hawk plopped down on a branch that was less than 15 feet up and relatively close.  Huntley Meadows Park, Alexandria, Virginia, USA 30NOV2023. #birds

Pacific Golden Plover seen on the Kona Coast of Hawaii. 

Pacific Golden Plovers breed in the summer months in Siberia and Alaska, and migrate south to Asia, Australasia, and Pacific islands for much of the winter. The Hawaiian population is well adapted to humans and are common park birds.

#birds

A bit of direct comparison of two of the gnatcatcher species in coastal California

The blue-gray gnatcatcher, wider range throughout continental US, has extensive white on the outer tail feathers

CA gnatcatchers have limited range to coastal SoCal and Baja CA and show only narrow white edges

📷🪶

#BirdGlamour’s Halloween-colored birds continues with Black-vented Orioles! These blackbird relatives call Central & South America home but are occasionally spotted in AZ & TX. Black-vented Orioles love an open area with scattered trees. Their name comes from their black vent (aka bum) area. 🐡🧪

Anna's Hummingbird 
           Relatively large-headed and chunky. Gray and green overall, lacking buffy or orangey tones.Males have shocking magenta head.
 Expanding its range northward into British Columbia, even regular in southeast Alaska. Winters to southern Baja and locally elsewhere in Mexico

After a week of chasing Parakeets on my bike, I’ve finally located the East Oxford parakeet roost. The local flock of 35-40 birds all roost in conifers just off Church Cowley Road, opposite the cemetery. Now, is there a north Oxford roost too?

Few wildlife spectacles epitomise the brutality of the natural world quite like the sight of a black-headed gull, carrying a croissant the size of its head, being chased by eight herring gulls over Morrison’s car park.

🧪🐡A purple-banded sunbird (Cinnyris bifasciatus) photographed in Maasai Mara, Kenya. Sometimes called “Africa’s hummingbirds”, sunbirds fulfill the same niche or ecological role as their hummingbird counterparts. They are specialized nectarivores and important pollinators.

Bird Glamour’s Glamager series continues with Hepatic Tanagers! This southern US to Central American dark red bird loves a pine-oak forest. These birds are little studied, but we do know they eat insects, spiders, and small fruits. Lady Hepatic Tanagers do most of the nest building. 🐡

A pair of Trumpeter Swans nests (dangerously close) to a road ~10 minutes from my house; I usually drive by and check on the cygnets every couple days. These little fluffballs become the largest native waterfowl in North America in no-time flat - the adults are 6+ feet long and weigh over 25 lbs 🪶

Welcome to the #BirdGlamour Glamager series, starting with the Western Tanager from western North America! The Western Tanager is extra and DOES NOT get his red coloration from a diet rich in carotenoids like many birds do: their red comes from rhodoxanthin from their insect-rich diet. 🐡🐦

Surfbird, a shorebird finely adapted to foraging on rocky coastlines in the surf zone, photographed today on the edge of San Francisco Bay.  Love the tiny water droplets beaded up on its back.

#birds

On the Hunt - Northern Water Shrew 🦋 
The North American Water Shrew is our smallest diving mammal at only 14-16cm long. It is primarily nocturnal, hunting in the pitch black of rivers and ponds by using its long whiskers to feel and blowing air bubbles which it then inhales to smell for prey.

I spent a small amount of time watching pacific ladder-backed — I mean… Nuttall’s woodpeckers… mating

After he finishes, he stays a while and strokes the back of her head a few times before they leave 

Such an intimate behavior to witness 🥰🪶

A great white egret wading thought an irrigated rice field at the estuary of the river Ebro, Catalunya. Despite invasive human intervention, the delta still teams with life and remains one of the main stopovers for flock of birds migrating from Europe to Africa.
📷 #photography #photocatalunya

Collared Incas are snazzy little hummingbirds. Most of the time when you see them, they look like they are wearing a black tuxedo. But if you catch them in just the right light, you can see some green to their feathers. 🧪🌿🪶
Tatama National Park, Colombia

Canada jays are quintessential northern birds - omnivorous, with thick fluffy plumage. They don't migrate, and breed in late winter (Feb/March) before arrival of spring migrants to their boreal forest home 🪶

Wildlife in my local #OnePlaceStudy. Fifteen species (butterflies, birds, wildflowers, dragonflies, grey squirrel, a fungus) caught on camera over the last couple of years at Fawsley in Northamptonshire, where I study the dead and photograph the living!

In May 2022 4 Glossy Ibis arrived on the Hampshire coast. They were mobile at first eventually preferring floods along the River Meon. In March, right image, they were still in winter plumage but by April they’d attained full summer. They moved on in summer but are back again now. #birds 🪶#ukbirding

This morning on the Boston side of the river, by Mass Ave and Storrow, I saw a young female bald eagle swoop into the Charles and then a second later it was flying over me with a fish in its talons. I have trouble with earnestness, but seeing bald eagles in the middle of the city is so fucking cool.

Gray Catbird numbers are way down from last week. Most of the pokeweed, Virginia creeper, and wild grape vines have been pretty well stripped of their fruits. I suspect this may be one of the last ones I see until next spring. Safe travels! #birds #fall #migration 🪶

🧪Rough day for that fish 😅 this is the pied kingfisher (Ceryle rudis), a species of cerylid aka water kingfisher. Cerylid kingfishers are true to their name and are specialized fish eaters. Photographed in Lake Naivasha, Kenya. I didn’t even notice the fish in its mouth until I was editing later!

The eared grebe has a piercingly red eye — totally freaky. In the fall almost all of them fly to Mono Lake or Great Salt Lake and pig out on brine shrimp and alkali flies, losing most of their flight ability and doubling in weight. They migrate at night. #Birds

💙 GM BlueSky! 💙

By their 3rd year, male Blue Grosbeaks acquire their full adult blue plumage. Regardless of age, males in particular often raise their crown feathers slightly when stimulated, giving the appearance of a small crest. 

Often?? I've only caught this 'crown' once! 😁

📷 P. caerulea 🪶🌿

Another forest bird I was extremely fortunate to see on my Kaua'i trip just over a year ago was this Puaiohi, a very rare thrush endemic to the highest elevations of the island. In addition to its harsh raspy calls, Puaiohi sing a song that essentially consists of their name 🪶
Red-throated Ant-tanager is a striking bird. It growls from bushes as you walk by. This one is a male. The females are russet gold and have some red on the throat also. #birding Belize

🔊👂 Southern ground hornbills are quite vocal! These social birds can inflate their brightly-colored throat pouches to let out loud, booming calls. This is done to announce location or attract a mate.

📷: Zookeeper Taylor S

Superb owl photo my wife took in 2016 in Brian Piccolo Park near Fort Lauderdale. The park is full of nesting burrowing owls who have no problem living among the people using the park for recreation, it’s really an amazing thing to see.

This palm warbler was one of a pair I saw zipping along near ground level, gleaning food. They eat primarily insects but will also eat seeds and berries. They also nest on the ground. Very un-warblery warbler! 🪶

Scaly-breasted lorikeet 🪶 dangling by one foot from a tree hollow. This bird and its mate were inspecting this as a possible nesting site. The upside-down entrance was a drawback, but not necessarily a dealbreaker. #ausbirds
Brisbane QLD

Not much in the way of rocky shores around here so the Surfbirds have to settle for mudflats has they travel to.from their alpine nesting sites. Anchorage Coastal Wildlife Refuge #Alaska Now on some rocky shoreline somewhere from Alaska to Chile (perhaps the longest but narrowest winter range).🪶🦑

🦅📉 Arizona's bald eagle population was reduced to three pairs in 1970! 😮‍💨

Due to elimination of DDT, habitat protection, & artificial reproduction efforts there were 69 breeding pairs, & a record 87 young bald eagles hatched during 2019. 📈

#ArtAdventCalendar Day 5: Bald Eagle, Lynx Lake, AZ 🪶🌿🐡

A snazzy Golden-naped Tanager pausing for a photo. These birds came be found in the Andean cloud forest from Colombia to Bolivia. They are omnivorous and dine on fruit and insects. 🌿🧪🪶
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

We have been hosting gracklepalooza at our place as more than a hundred had gathered in preparation for their journey south. Most have moved on but we still have quite a few. This male common grackle was foraging in the trees  and really showing off the colours!

First river trip of the year, in February of all months… took Kayak Horseman out from Lathrop Homes down to just past the Cortland bridge. Spotted a pile of trash that looked like a heron then saw this prehistoric, gigantic-mosquito-looking dinosaur-ass birb fly out from behind it.
Lark sparrow in the desert. I see them with the large flocks of cute little Brewer’s sparrows and abundant White-crowned sparrows that have the good sense to spend the winter in the desert.  🪶

The level of fluffiness of this anhinga resting on a tree is amazing. As their feather coat is not waterproof (like ducks), I saw most of them sunbathing with spread wings soaked in water.

But this one was already air-dried in the warm sun of Costa Rica 
🪶

We spotted this handsome Barred Fruiteater skulking around and at first thought it was a thrush. These birds are true to their name and only eat fruit. They can be found in the Andes Mountains from Venezuela south to Bolivia. Tatamà National Park, Colombia 🧪🌿🪶

Bird irises come in a rainbow of colours – but what are the causes behind them?

Some ideas, and a call for more data gathering and research, over on the British Ornithologist’s Union blog.

#Birds
#Ornithology

bou.org.uk/blog-corbett...

Superb Lyrebird outside my window! There were actually two of them, but the other one was a little further back in the bushes. Here’s a 10-second video of it having a scratch. drive.google.com/file/d/1QIsc...

Remember when we were talking about the difference between the red spots & beak lengths on downy and hairy woodpeckers? 🪶
1 - downy (single wide red patch) 
2- hairy (two red spots) 
3- downy (cute tiny beak) 
4- hairy (tree-smashing jackhammer beak)
Golden-crowned Sparrow, once called a Miner Bird in California because its call was said to commiserate "No gold here!" #birding Whitaker Ponds, Portland, Oregon

Thinking about how cute bowerbird courtship behavior is. Males build their little structures because the ladies like them, young males stop by when the adult isn't around to practice, and they like to collect pretty things. Amazing. 

Pic is a Great Bowerbird bower in Far North Queensland, 2019 🌿🪶

From today’s birding at the Hassayampa River Preserve near Wickenburg, AZ:
1) my first-ever ladder-backed woodpecker
2) a black phoebe in good lighting
3) a male Anna’s hummingbird with the magenta head happening
4) a male vermilion flycatcher 🪶

The terror of Montezuma Rainforest Lodge. This little Rufous-tailed Hummingbird liked to hide in the bushes an attempt to chase off any other hummingbird in his territory.
Tatamà National Park, Colombia 🧪🌿🪶

Birdfeeder Drama:

Last week our lone blue-jay learned that mimicking a hawk will cause every squirrel at the feeder to dive for cover.

Just now he tried it on the doves and is visibly perplexed that it didn't fucking work, so he flew down close and tried again.

Then the doves beat his ass.

I grew up in a pretty competitive birding environment, mostly friendly but occasionally a little catty

I just love the Champaign Illinois birding crew in comparison. Folks helping each other find and ID birds, crediting others by name in rare eBird reports, so pleasant

A dull day travelling along the Niagara Parkway next to the Niagara River (Canada-side) brightened instantly by a gaggle of at least 55 delightful migrating trumpeter swans swimming along almost single file & engaged in their customary honking. 🪶#birds #waterfowl

Open water on the lakes and ponds around Anchorage #Alaska is getting pretty scarce but Common Goldeneyes can get by with surprisingly little water. A few usually manage to stick it out after most other ducks have bailed on us. 🪶

The Gold-ringed Tanager has the local common name Bangsia de Tatama for the very limited range where it is found. The tanager is the symbol of Tatama National Park and a species that has inspired conservation in Colombia. 🪶🌿

I've been fascinated with 🪶 Boat-billed Herons as long as I've known they existed, it was a privilege to observe this mating pair discussing suitable nesting sites this morning in Tortuguero National Park, Costa Rica

Black Phoebe. These birds have a long range - from the western US south all the way to Argentina. They are primarily insectivorous. If you love birds, gotta conserve bugs. 🌿🧪🪶
Tatama National Park, Colombia

Check out this really incredible example of xanthochroism in a warbler. This is a condition of excess yellow color deposition in the feathers, but in this case the naturally melanic patterns seem to be overriding the excess yellow, making for a gorgeous bird 🪶
macaulaylibrary.org/asset/163805...

1/ The Slaty Flowerpiercer is endemic to Costa Rica, found in mountains above 1300m, and is one of 18 flowerpiercer species found only in Central and South America. #birds

Slaty Flowerpiercer 
(Diglossa plumbea)
November 25, 2024
Suenos Del Bosque, San José, CR

🧪Check out this bald eagle migration in Harrison Mills, Canada 🦅 Each fall, bald eagles flock to the Harrison/Fraser Rivers, drawn by the return of spawning salmon. I expected a lot of eagles, but was not emotionally prepared to see them just casually walking around everywhere like pigeons in NYC 🙃

i saw bald eagles today. on january first, in canada. they made their gay little chitters (no hawk-like shrieks as USian propaganda would have you believe), sitting on trees, contending with one another, flapping their mighty wings. they are beautiful animals.

Oystercatcher searching for food in rocks exposed during low tide along shoreline of San Francisco Bay in Emeryville, California.  Early sundown for first photo and sun has almost set in second. (I saw an Oystercatcher years ago and it took another year or so to see another.)
#Birds 🦉
#Photography 📷

Ladder-backed Woodpeckers (Dryobates scalaris) are native to the SW US and MX. They can be inconspicuous and quiet, requiring a bit of time and patience to find. Their small size and agility make them deft foragers.

Woodpecker Wednesday! 🪶🌿

Flies, fabulous flies. Estimates vary, but there are about 1 million fly species in the world and only around 14% of them have been described. Here are few gems from my little patch. 🧪

Black Scavenger Fly (Sepsis sp.), Marsh Fly (Dicta sp.) and Tachinid Fly.

Today's #101CuriousTales concerns the Woodpigeon, one of Europe's most numerous #birds. Such high numbers of a farmland bird makes these a major pest species. In the UK Woodpigeons are resident, but here & elsewhere in Europe huge numbers will soon be moving south.  1/N 🧪🌎🌿

Masked Trogons are common throughout the Andes cloud forests. These birds eat fruit and they also catch flying insects. This male was busy looking around for a meal.
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador 🪶🌿🧪

A Great Blue Heron from the Anchorage Coastal Wildlife Refuge #Alaska (last month). An everyday bird for many of you, well those in North America, but we are at the very edge of the range and lucky to get a sighting a year.

Pale-billed Flowerpecker seen on the IIT Bombay campus, Mumbai, Maharashtra, India.

They're hard birds to get a good look at: Tiny (only 8 cm long), and they spend most of their time foraging for fruits and flowers (often from epiphytes and mistletoes) in the tree canopy. 

#birds

🧪Meet the Steller's sea eagle (Haliaeetus pelagicus), among the largest of all eagle species by both weight and wingspan! This individual is one half of a mated pair being cared for by Woodland Park Zoo as part of one of the U.S.’s few conservation breeding programs for this species 🦅
Abert’s towhee, foraging for goodies among the ground litter in Arizona. Spends most of its time on the ground, in fact. 🪶

American Kestrel

We found this pretty female murderbird along a roadside near Nanegalito, Ecuador. American Kestrels have a large range, from most of North American south into the western portion of South America and then throughout most of the southern portion of the continent.
🪶🌿🧪
It’s not every Monday morning I’m woken by four surprisingly large water buffalo drinking and relieving themselves in the pond by my shepherd’s hut. (Coombeshead Rewilding Centre, Devon).
This Black-billed Magpie didn't have time to stop and chat. Beak full of bug, he gave me serious side-eye as he strolled past. #Birds  Westchester Lagoon, Anchorage, AK

A great part of Sayulita, Mexico: there's one patch of the Pacific Ocean that apparently is filled with the dumbest fish. Over and over brown pelicans speared into the water, shooting out their heads at the last moment like a snake striking to snatch fish from the water. #Mexico #Birds #Birding

The pyrrhuloxia or desert cardinal is a bird that inhabits the arid region from the southwestern United States to central Mexico.
It is similar to the northern cardinal in terms of song and behavior, and both coexist in many desert areas.

Since you’re enjoying the red-winged blackbird pic (& name), here are some tricolored blackbirds. Diff species. Found mostly in CA. Grassland specialists. Endangered.

Similar to red-winged, but males have white wing patch (not orange/yellow) and females are darker with clearer eyebrow stripe.

Kāruhiruhi fledglings on nest. Kāruhiru is the Māori name for the pied cormorant or shag. Photos taken at the Chelsea Heritage Park in Tāmaki-Makarau (Auckland). NZ has 12 of the 18 cormorant species, that exist globally. 
#addBirder
#birds

Honing photography skills picking off wary warblers certainly helps with flighty rainforest birds. This handsome friend is a Common Tody-Flycatcher and it never stopped moving! 
Montezume Rainforest Lodge, Colombia
🧪🌿🪶

Greater Yellowlegs and its little buddy Lesser Yellowlegs giving a nice rare comparison this evening at Steigerwald Lake in  Washougal, Washington. Greater is not interested in sharing its salamander. #birding

Barnegat part 11B: Harlequin Ducks edition continued. At Barnegat, these ducks like to hang out on the inlet side of the jetty, fairly close to the jetty walls. When they’ve shown up locally, they tend to hang out by our breakwalls along the beach, where Lake Erie tends to be the roughest. 🪶

Western meadowlark in front of a great egret over a vernal pool! 

Earlier this year, we had record rain in Southern California. Our local park has  vernal pools with fairy shrimp! 

An abundant water year made it possible for the scene to happen as western meadowlark’s winter in on our bluff￼ 🪶

Gang-gang Cockatoos! These endangered beauties are roughly the size of Galahs and limited to a small area of coastal South-Eastern Australia. The local flock visited our garden yesterday, and I counted *sixteen* of them!

Great egrets: masters of the art of striking a pose. With a slow, sassy strut, they wade through the waters in pursuit of their aquatic prey. Showcasing their prowess as ambush predators, these birds are striking in more than one way! Nature’s choreography unfolds in these feathered maestros.

I also heard weird noises coming from my chimney. It sounded like the crunching of bones.
I thought it was migratory birds at first, so two years ago I set up a sound-activated camera looking up the chimney to capture the source. It took this photo.

Bird of the day.. Waxwing (Bombycilla garrulus) We’ve had quite the irruption of these bandit masked bounty hunters this year! Our locale is blessed with juniper, rowan & mistletoe berries. They now move on to orchards new.. a joy all told these past few weeks..🪶

A Montane Woodcreeper working on a tasty moth. The bird is bashing the moth on the tree to knock off the scales on the moth's wings. These birds hitch up trees like woodpeckers while foraging for insects. 🪶🌿🧪
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

A stunning Scarlet-Bellied Mountain Tanager working on some feather care - the bird was happily bathing. These fruit eating birds can be found in the high forests of the Andes Mountains from Venezuela to Bolivia. 🌿🧪🪶🐡
Zuroloma Birding, Nono, Ecuador
A great blue heron about to devour a crayfish.  Taken just a few days ago, 3-Oct-2023 in Sterling, Virginia, USA.

The resident Kingfisher at Rye Harbour Nature Reserve has been catching some rays of sunshine in the saltmarsh opposite the Discovery Centre. Amazing glimpses of metallic copper and cobalt blue.

sussexwildlifetrust.org.uk/news/kingfis...

📷 Barry Yates
This Broad-billed Motmot has evidence of its nest burrowing on its bill. It was busy digging until I rudely interrupted it this morning at Tapir Valley, Costa Rica.

When birding in your own garden in South Africa gets interesting. A pair of African Hoopoes successfully raised a Greater Honeyguide. 
#Southern African birds #birding

saw a bald eagle while clearing privet. as common as they are now it’s still a thrill.

Also, the deer are using the newly-cleared area for bedding down, which is validating
Various waterbirds, taken from a great distance during our yurt stay last week: red-necked grebe, common loon, red-breasted mergansers. All perfectly at home on the aptly-named Bird Lake in Manitoba.

I love birding as a way of connecting with Nature; cycling is also something that is very important as a way to wind down. Last year, I decided to combine them with an Oxfordshire 'Big Year' by bike - here's a write up of the 177 species that I saw:
oxonbirding.blogspot.com/2024/01/an-o...

It's winter (weird duck season) in the Northern hemisphere, so here are two of the weird Australian ducks 🪶 from this area. Plumed whistling ducks - they whistle but don't quack - and pink-eared ducks, aka "zebra shovellers"
Brisbane, QLD
##ausbirds

Nice mixed flock of mostly White-eyed Parakeets (Psittacara leucophthalmus) with a couple of Red-masked Parakeets (Psittacara erythrogenys) feeding on the fruit of a Bullet Tree (Terminalia buceras), just outside my window 

#MiamiWildlife 🪶

If you love birds, you need to conserve bugs. In this BugShot - Birding crossover event we have a Lemon-browed Flycatcher with a katydid. The bird say there with it so it was probably waiting for us to leave so it could head to its nest. 🌿🪶#inverts

It's about as far from the sea as you can get in the UK, and in the crowded south-east, but the diversity of birds around Oxford is just brilliant. Some of the highlights of the 167 species I recorded within 10km of home in 2023. No apologies for some distant record shots - it's birding!

A lone Kentish Plover flying with Sanderling. 
Since 2007, the breeding population in Spain of the KP has dropped by 52% to an estimated 3,300 pairs. Heavy tourism, beach cleaning, extreme temperatures & dogs off leads are some causes. 🪶
This half-ounce blackpoll warbler undertakes an epic migration journey - from the boreal forest north of here to spend winter in South America. It didn't stay long in my garden 🪶

One of the easiest, best things we've ever done for your yard is just deciding to let most of it be. We had a slim dead tree that fell towards the back last year, and this log has been a focal point for all kinds of wildlife, including this male Pileated Woodpecker this morning. 🪶
iconic Australian landscape - superb parrots at their nest hollow in an old red gum, with kangaroos grazing in the shade

Warning: R-rated content. Avert your eyes! 😄
Good to see the resident pied oystercatchers at Bald Hill Beach working on their family planning. They have been so unlucky. Every year they try. Every year they fail to become parents. Maybe this time?
🪶 #birds

The wiki entry for Say's phoebe says, "it prefers dry, desolate areas." So, like, a very popular beach. 😁

Natural Bridges SB, Santa Cruz, CA

Sunday afternoon went birding with a friend in South Louisiana. As the sun was setting we noticed a large amount of Scissor-tailed Flycatchers. A careful 1x1 count and got us 53! As we were leaving they started to roost in the Oak trees. Quite a sight just an hour south of New Orleans!
Warbler migration is ramping up. Fall warblers can be hard to identify, but thankfully black-and-white warblers are always recognizable. 🪶

One of my favorite local birding patches is a park with a mowed lawn, a few gigantic trees, and a shrub-lined creek

It's a great migrant trap, those shrubs get loaded with warblers

Today, though, there was also a gorgeous Barred Owl in the lawn trees, my first there! (photo taken from a distance)

Since I’ve been having some lovely conversations about American folk horror, today’s book recommendation is Maynard’s House by Herman Raucher (1980).  A Vietnam vet with PTSD “inherits” a house from a fallen soldier in a remote area of Maine and decides to hole up there for the winter

FIREBUGS by Nino Bulling is a fantastic existential-crisis-right-beneath-the-surface book; Bulling's cartooning is at the same time sparse and explosive, perfectly fitting a story whose silences feel like drowning more than anything else. Glad D&Q is giving this book a wider release.

Today's book rec is Emil Ferris' MY FAVORITE THING IS MONSTERS. Her haunting work grapples with childhood, family trauma, the political crises of the 1960s, and the legacy of the Holocaust, drawn in a style that feels like a fresh amalgam of Robert Crumb, pulp horror, Lynda Barry, and George Grosz.

If you’re ever worried the thing you’re writing is too “out there,” just remember that Philip K Dick’s semi-autobiographical novel VALIS about a guy named Horselover Fat who gets shot w/ a pink laser beam that contains the wisdom of the gnostic God has 28,824 ratings on Goodreads

Finished LeGuin's The Dispossessed. The story of a space communist visiting a capitalist planet and the psychic horror of experiencing capitalism for the first time. At times it's more manifesto than narrative, but what a manifesto. One of the most thought-provoking sci-fi novels I've ever read.

Rereading JESUS’ SON again—because I always am—and marveling again at the incredible efficiency of this opening. A single paragraph of setup to establish the ground situation, then a “but” to break it. I’d spend three pages doing this work, and to lesser effect.

If you’ve never heard of His Master’s Voice, by Stanisław Lem, it’s probably one of my favourite works of science fiction, mostly because it’s just a carefully-written work of philosophy of science with absolute bangers on every page. I’d call it epistemic cosmic horror

I just read FORGET THIS EVER HAPPENED by Cassandra Rose Clarke and couldn't believe it wasn't on my radar before. '90s-set small town summer wlw romance w/a mesmerizing paranormal mystery with time & fate itself as stakes + monsters who aren't monsters

Just finished @@346220 's MAD SISTERS OF ESI and it's magnificent, a tale of tales wrapped in iridescent spacetime, an infinitely nested creation myth, a portal and a ship and a campfire woven of prose. Reminded me of Calvino, and Clarke's PIRANESI (but spacefaring).

Currently reading the late Michael Shea's MR. CANNYHARME, which went unpublished in his lifetime. But damn is it amazing, with exquisite writing, Lovecraftian menace, and a street-level tour of San Francisco's Mission District.
Reading my second-ever T. Kingfisher book, Thornhedge, and this might be the best thing I've ever read: "thorns die from the inside out, like priests"

Un des livres, si ce n'est le plus important que j'ai lu pour comprendre Wakefield et le mouvement antivax actuel. C'est limpide. Deer est un journaliste fantastique et son livre explique avec méthode que la plupart du temps, tout ce qui compte c'est la reconnaissance et le pognon. Pas les patients.

Recommended read: The Blue, Beautiful World by Karen Lord. Stellar example of SF exploring the human condition with insight and compassion in a dramatic tale of ordinary people trying to cope when their small planet is caught between powerful alien races who may decide humanity’s fate is irrelevant.

Starling House by Alix E. Harrow.

A gorgeous gothic novel with a haunted house at its center and metatextual footnotes. I love how this story explores how history is created and our understanding of the past, how the stories we tell become what's true.

I should have read this decades ago. Extraordinarily daring for its time, in its depiction of sexuality, age and feminism, it's also a brilliantly-paced, multi-viewpoint story, which races the reader along. Marvellous. It's early days, but it's probably my book of the year.

I began my week with this wondrous book, a needed balm when the world hurts and I’m drowning in work. It is a masterful novella that packs a sophisticated and deeply affecting reflection on the historical layers of loss in a text of perfectly distilled economy. A most deserved Nobel.

Everyone stop scrolling and go buy THE SAINT OF BRIGHT DOORS by @@64002 RIGHT NOW

Weird fantasy, bleak horror, the absurdism of religion, race, caste, bureaucracy, and capitalism. The loneliness of parental abuse. All told with Vajra's sharp and beautiful prose. One of my top reads this year.

Reading Skin Thief by Suzan Palumbo.  Propagating Peonies might be the best story about the deep ambivalence at the heart of Buddhist practice I’ve ever read. This book is so ridiculously good. Deeply embodied stories about the spiritual homelessness of being pulled between 2 worlds

I was always going to read Rachel Nuwer's MDMA book 
'I feel Love', but it really has exceeded my expectations. It's fascinating and informative, not only on the history and current state of psychedelic science as a whole, but on the nature of trauma. Warmly recommended.

Legends and Lattes by Travis Baldree is for everyone who's already living in the year 3000 when their OCs have retired from saving the world and finally opened up that quaint downtown cafe they've been dreaming about.
love reading Auden because you get sheer devastation like "ghosts must do again / what brings them pain," then the goofiest possible use of the word "sexy"

Currently reading an ARC of @@208442's A MASK OF FLIES, and let me tell you, this is thing is SOLID. Great pacing, dialogue, violence, remarkable sense of place. Perfectly poised between that sweet spot of crime and horror. SO GOOD. us.macmillan.com/books/978125...

DEAD SET is probably my least known novel. It’s about a young punk—Zoe—who discovers her dead dad’s soul on an LP in a strange record shop, but loses it, and must travel to the underworld to save him. Almost no one has heard of the book, but it means a lot to me.

I’ve spent the evening with Essex Hemphill’s 1992 poetry and prose collection, Ceremonies. Dealing with black masculinity and queerness, the AIDS epidemic, kink and sex, and essays on the problem of Robert Mapplethorpe and the joy of Paris is Burning. Highly recommend

The greatest living ghost story writer is surely Reggie Oliver, who not only writes well, but also in a variety of styles including pastiches. He’s quite remarkable. Much of his work can be purchased through Tartarus Press.

Having slept on it I'm confident saying that The Malazan Book of the Fallen is the best fantasy series I have ever read. Completely engrossed me from start to finish. I read ten books, 1200 pages a pop, in four months. I could not put them down. The easiest recommendation I've ever given. Read 'em!

FRIENDS AND BOOKWYRMS, I NEED ASSISTANCE.

Give me your favorite high fantasy.
But do not give me the following:
Tolkien
Rothfuss
BrandoSando
GRR Martin
Robert Jordan

And do not give me scifi. I can tell the difference.
I need like 3 or 4 titles. Let's see if you pick what I've already got.

I’m shifting my 2023 reading thread from Twitter with #50, which is Room Temperature by Nicholson Baker. Baker is the apotheosis of James’s writer “on whom nothing is lost” and he turns what should be a curse of King Midas proportions (Remember and be forced to share every detail!) into a blessing.

PINK SLIME by Fernanda Trías (Heather Cleary transl.) is a sombre ecological catastrophe novel. The narrator lives in a coastal city infested with a toxic form of algae. The fish have died, the rich have moved away, and the protagonist suffers through isolation, depression and a family drama.

After finishing BRIDGE by @@179489, I screamed out loud and then lay motionless, hugging the book. What a breathtaking, masterful, relentless tale of grief and messy mother-daughter relationships and multiverse-hopping parasites. Please read it.

laurenbeukes.com/books/bridge

If you see this, post a book you’ve read more than once.

Spare, bleak, and brutal, WE WHO ARE ABOUT TO… demolishes many of the village pieties of genre SF. It’s also very funny. 

It was originally published in GALAXY during the editorship of Jim Baen. Which is to say, people are complicated.

I've heard many books described as "something meets Twin Peaks." James Kennedy's Bride of the Tornado is the first book I've read that lives up to that description. I'm about halfway through and it's so weird and unsettling and a little funny and romantic, and I'm loving it.

Gatecrashing my Books of the Year list is this masterful piece from @@22511, an "anti-memoir" full of dense, intricate pieces about writing and life that  have the common aim of forcing you to look at the world in a different way. Complex, thought-provoking, top-quality stuff.

Okay, I think i'm gonna wrap this thread up with a favorite of mine. It's arguably not horror, but deeply horrifying, so

It's Ken Russell's The Devils. I've never seen a better treatise on power and the church. It's scathing, it's gorgeous, and it's got hot priest Oliver Reed 🥵

The Saint of Bright Doors by @@64002 is such a unique & great book about the son of a messiah he was raised to kill. I was sucked in by the complex & realistic post-colonial but still oppressive world, fleshed out characters finding their way in it, & really 🤯 use of the narrator’s POV. 🔥

I've read a lot of Matheson that I haven't liked, but the reputation I AM LEGEND has of being one of the best horror novels, and one of the best science fiction novels, and according to some one of the best novels of any kind, is completely deserved. A once-in-a-lifetime idea, perfectly executed.
the Tiffany Aching books (starting with The Wee Free Men) are also a good recommendation if you're more into YA stuff (they're also some of Pratchett's best work in general)

JR Drunk On All Your Strange New Words by Eddie Robson. Very good, extremely well-plotted and well-written, with memorable and vivid characters. I have to say it reminded me a lot of... nothing I have ever read before because it was so startlingly imaginative and ingenious. Highly recommended.

Picked up Alexievich’s elegant, brutal, heart-breaking, and image-filled history, told as interwoven oral histories, of Soviet women who served in WWII, from laundresses to snipers, and did not put it down until I finished.

I finished Jaime Hernandez's MAGGIE THE MECHANIC, and it wasn't at all what I expected but still a pleasure. It's honestly kind of shocking how little friction there is, in terms of Hernandez finding his footing; it's perfectly established, immensely assured but never "showy."

In one of my audiobook groups someone asked for “weirdest/grossest/most disturbing” horror novels. Some people are suggesting Dean Koontz and James Patterson. I mean, I guess experiences may vary. I just sent them a link to @@62103’s Splattepunk awards.
If you are a space person, you are going to want to read Samantha Harvey's ORBITAL, a remarkable evocation of life on the International Space Station. Stunning, beautiful, profound.

finished reading 'Saint Of Bright Doors' by Vajra Chandrasekera. 

You need to read this. One of the best books I've read this year. Chandrasekera created an incredibly messy and meaty world full of terror and religious wonder, with beautiful prose that doesn't let up for the entire book.

No Shelter But The Stars by Virginia Black, for a story that primarily takes place on a barren rock or in space, this novel is so viscerally lush with emotional depth! Even the most spartan passages hit so that you feel it with your whole chest. Just a hugely satisfying read.

Sapphic books | WLW

Piranesi was powerful enough to haunt me with dreams of stone halls, with sea and statues and mysterious messages, their chalk half-wiped away. 

Immediately one of my favourite books ever. I can still hear and feel it.

The Saint of Bright Doors reads like a novel-length short story in the best way possible: full of fresh ideas, fast-moving, with an evident care applied to each and every sentence. Remarkable and highly-recommended.

Weekly book talk for Saturday, February 4. What’s everyone reading?

If I had the power to compel 330 million Americans to read one and only one book, it would be The Autobiography of Malcolm X (1965).

Let's talk pretty writing in fantasy. I'm thinking of books like Erin Morgenstern's The Starless Sea, The Ten Thousand Doors of January by Alix E Harrow, The Necessity of Rain by Sarah Chorn, and Piranesi by Susanna Clarke. Who would you add? Share your favorites!

Finished Solenoid by Mircea Cartarescu, tr Sean Cotter. A battle at times, but worth the effort I think. A richly imaginative stream/waterslide of consciousness slipping between dream states and reality for a nobody Romanian school teacher. Lots of great lines and tough paragraphs.

I am very happy to learn the French edition of my 2016 novel REVOLVER won this year's Rivages Booksellers' Prize, which is chosen by a jury of 96 booksellers in France, Chile, Morocco and Quebec! A high-five to my brilliant translator, Sophie Aslanides, for her amazing work.

Finished Farthings by Charles Boyle – 113 short pieces (a para each) about life as a one man publishing house CBEditions. I enjoyed it enormously. Like Alan Bennett's diaries only with a sharper edge. Miraculously free of industry bitterness, but lots of wry insights about how publishing works.

I have finished reading the latest @@4966 book, What Feasts at Night, and can tell you that the author is one of the best writers of the experience of having a nightmare that I have ever read. Just, captures the fuckin' WEIRDNESS of bad dreams.

A++ 
10/10
No notes.

I woke up early and couldn’t get back to sleep, so read the brilliant Sexcastle by @@144959 

It’s a riot from start to finish, about an assassin trying to live a peaceful life, until…

If you dig classic action movies, John Wick, Road House etc. it’s a must!

He Who Drowned The World by @@166678 is brilliant, staggering, and hurtful. It, even more than SWBTS, is an incredibly nuanced exploration of gender in an excellent political fantasy. It is brutal, but it has a light shining through. Just fantastic.

Announcing the winner of the 2023 Cercador Prize: @@241041’s translation of Ana Paula Maia's OF CATTLE AND MEN.

The Cercador committee says OF CATTLE AND MEN "lays bare the brutal labor of the slaughterhouse trade," hailing the novel as a "new breed of western."

Incredible little work, this - like Ford’s WELL-BUILT CITY by way of WE HAVE ALWAYS LIVED IN THE CASTLE. One of the most effectively moving finales I’ve ever read. Remarkably consonant with MOTHTOWN. What a time to be alive and reading books about death.

I’ve been reading Discworld for the first time this year and I cannot overstate how good it is. It’s funny, smart, and has just layers of “oh that’s a good point…” that makes you think and hopefully grow.

I wish I had read them sooner.

Absolutely blown away by C. J. Leede's MAEVE FLY, which sets itself higher and higher hurdles and then backflips over every one of them. It invites so many comparisons veteran authors would quake at, but it earns them. Mean, nasty, solipsistic, amazing.

The Case of Cem by Bulgarian writer Vera Mutafchieva deserves to be part of the 20th century canon of global literature. Now, thanks to International Booker winner Angela Rodel, Anglophone readers can discover this epic novel. These are finished copies. Let me know if you want an ARC.

Just finished Malcolm Lowry’s first novel, Ultramarine. A bit purple, and fascinating regarding how much a writer insists on getting out of the “will he cheat on his lady back home question” and whether or not he will get in a fight with the ship’s resident tough dude. Not a bad intro to Lowry.
if you like heists and crooks who aren’t very good at being crooks, i’m begging you to read donald e. westlake’s Dortmunder novels

Finished The Sarah Book by Scott McClanahan. An unpasteurised study of the feral grief of relationship & personal breakdown. Simultaneously one of the funniest & saddest books I have ever read. One of the best too. I'm used to book sadness, book poignancy, book emotions – but this is the real thing.

Today's book rec is Paul Beatty's THE SELLOUT, which possesses in great measure what all powerful satires possess: beneath its comedic madcap zaniness there is a scratching, a tearing; an overwhelming sense of the terror of history bubbling up from below; a profound and wholly justified cosmic rage.

Just finished my 10th book of the year with a re-read of The Blade Itself, which is still as incredible as it was when I first read it 18 (🤮) years ago. Really looking forward to revisiting the series, and then reading A Little Hatred and the sequels for the first time.

Happy New Year! Starting Pedro Páramo by Juan Rulfo, translated from the Spanish by Douglas J. Weatherford. First book of the year. For newly formed novella book club. And because Sontag called it “one of the masterpieces of twentieth-century world literature.”

Happy #PubDay to this mighty, mighty book. 

In 1997, Sarabande published LAST CALL, a poetry anthology that became a formative text on the lived experiences of addiction. Now, more than 25 years later, editors Kaveh Akbar and Paige Lewis offer this companion volume for a new generation.

Am reading The Little Rebel, by French author Jérôme Leroy and it is amazing. The story of tensions and terror in a French city, it’s immensely powerful and thought-provoking. It’s only 80 pages and we’ll worth a read.

PROMISE is another SUPERB batch of strange, eerie, unearthly stories from @@4103588. Weird futures, fantastical technologies, and unusual histories populate these worlds where Reality shimmers like a mirage of a dream: it may seem similar to ours, but it's never quite the same.

This is a terrific novel. Doherty was from Scotland but worked as a miner near Rotherham - he's really important in that tradition of post-war working-class Yorkshire fiction that leads to Stan Barstow, John Braine, David Storey and the rest.

Semi-recently read Poe's PYM and Mat Johnson's PYM back to back and I highly recommend this, the experience is absolutely hilarious. I was going to write an essay about it but frankly, Johnson's book kind of incorporates that essay already. If I do write about this, it's gonna be about shoggoths tho

Book 1 of 2024 is done. Dexter & Sinister Detecting Agents by Keith W. Dickinson.

A fun steampunk murder mystery with a talking cat. Can you ask for more? Well, okay, Dexter is not a 'real' cat, as he is quick to point out, but that just makes him more sarcastic.

In December 1980, Vivian Stephens launched a new line of contemporary category romance at Dell: Candlelight Ecstasy. 

The line pushed the envelope when it came to sex & sensuality on page.

But how sexy are they? And how do the books hold up in 2023?
#romancebks #romancescholarship

Winner of the 2023 Booker, deserves all the accolades. I'm not sure I've ever read a book that affected my physically like Paul Lynch's Profit Song. I had to put it down at times. I had to remember to breathe. The anxiety-inducing story of Ireland's fall into fascism is simply brilliant.

Reading Vaults of Vaarn and indie rpg companies keep proving why they deserve more attention than WotC. This book is a condensed, tight rpg with a fascinating setting and incredible tools that you could play in every week until you die all in less than 150 pages. 10/10.

reading Manhunt this evening by Gretchen Felker-Martin, & one thing that strikes me is how beautiful everything is. The way she describes the settings, the world, the fog, the mist, the burned out buildings. There are tender moments, & beautiful, & when combined w the violence & terror it's dizzying
BookTok, I'm watching all these "top books I read in 2023" and so many of you are putting out all or mostly white lists and I really need you to try harder to read diversely.

This horror novel I'm reading takes place in a haunted house and half the haunting effects of the house is it makes the women wanna have lesbian sex with each other. This is presented as a sign of evil and horror and its very very funny. Oh 1970

Hallucinations by Oliver Sacks is the best book I have read on the subject. Informative. Empathetic. Scientific but an easy read. I have nothing but respect for this man’s work. Hallucinating is a fascinating part of the human experience.

No better way to spend a Sunday than reading a book from cover to cover, and few better books to read from cover to cover than this one. The humour, lightness of touch and spirited voice somehow both leaven and highlight the horrific poverty that she endured. Thanks for the recommendation, Bluesky!

i read 3 james m cain novels last night bc i couldn't sleep and i tell ya the famousest ones (double indemnity, the postman always rings twice) are way less good than mildred pierce, which is Very Very Very Good Indeed

today's lifting weights, in case i need to bodyslam a 🐷, is brought to you by:

Nadifa Mohamed's incredible biographical novel 'Black Mamba Boy'

and

'Master Slave Husband Wife: An Epic Journey from Slavery to Freedom' (non-fiction) by Ilyon Woo

both are great and essential reading 💙📚

I read an excellent novel (The Round House by Louise Erdrich) cover to cover in one very long sitting yesterday and am still gently buzzing with it. It’s the very best way to read a novel, when you can make the time. I wish I could do it more often.

It’s time. APPARITIONS, my Deaf-centred prairie gothic horror novel, is now loosed upon the world. 

It’s the most personal book I’ve ever written, but as Martin Scorsese says, the most personal is the most creative. May it find its way into the hands of those who need it.🤟🏼

MAEVE FLY is dark, perverse, and incredibly violent. It’s a new era of AMERICAN PSYCHO with a gut punch ending. There are secrets lurking in the basement, and they are drenched in blood. It all comes together with the perfect climax that you don’t see coming until it’s too late. I loved this book.🕸️📚
If you're all all into fantasy or weird little guys or just good prose you should read the Gormenghast series, incredible books that are still much less well-known in the US than LotR etc.

THE HANDYMAN METHOD is one of the creepiest books I’ve read in a while. It’s a Nick Cutter novel so it’s also very squishy and gross. The run up to the ending is a series of mind-blowing revelations that expose the reader to dark pacts, familial secrets, and a finale full of consequence. 📚🕸️

I’m a sucker for meta-fiction, and Rich Chizmar’s CHASING THE BOOGEYMAN was a masterpiece in that regard. The beauty of the follow-up, BECOMING THE BOOGEYMAN is that I can’t 100% confirm this text message is fiction. (Can’t speak to the reality of @@3114’s text).

Finished “Neuromancer” and really liked it.  It feels both promethean and fully realized.  Worth it for the swagger and imagery alone.  Idk that I’m going to become a Gibson head the way I am with Pynchon, but I’ll probably at least do “Mona Lisa Overdrive” at some point cause I like Molly Millions.

This morning I finished reading Catriona Ward's LOOKING GLASS SOUND, and it was an absolute wonder of a book.

Did doubt that any American man in the history of America has ever opened a carton of watercress sauce, but maybe that was just another meta-treat in a book filled with them.

This is the best book about space exploration I've read in a very long time, and if you, too, dream of exploring space without exploiting it, then you, too, will end up with your copy festooned with Post-it notes. physicsworld.com/a/towering-e...

I'm currently reading an early proof of The Silverblood Promise, the debut novel by James Logan (JFB, April 2024). Oh man, if you liked The Lies of Locke Lamora you're going to *love* this. 

The pyramid game is one of the most sadistic things I think I've ever read; worthy of Hellraiser, almost.
whoever was looking for book recommendations, "canticle for liebowitz" is the best book I have ever read, a position it has held since I first encountered it almost four decades ago

I love paying honor to my Blackness through jokes, stories and the oral tradition.

That said, this a book that I *always* read. It's a compendium of Black folklore going all the way back to Africa. It's always relevant, thought-provoking, and it's the funniest damn book in the world.

Just finished Lucifer by Dan Watters, Max Fiumara, Sebastian Fiumara et al. 

Couldn’t hit my sweet spot more if it tried. Gorgeous art, great story and definitely a strong compliment to the wider Sandman world.

finally read this essay and it's really good. But in mirroring my own memories of this time, a lot of the New Weird was heavily dude-coded. Indeed my favorite novel of that milieu The Etched City by KJ Bishop was never mentioned! (You should read it) textjournal.scholasticahq.com/article/1856...

Wonderful Sunday reading: this Mexican novel, a fragmentary non-chronological Bildungsroman based on Yukio Mishima’s travels to Mexico and the US as well as his formation as a writer. One of the best Mexican books I’ve read recently. I hope it makes it to translation

I have just finished Bombshell by Sarah MacLean, and it is--to me-- the platonic ideal of a romance novel. Banter! Smexy! A thick thighed hero who's got big Roy Kent vibes! A heroine who's proficient in explosions! I giggled REGULARLY.

Books of 2023. One of the two or three best debuts of 2023, Some Desperate Glory takes the age old stuff of space opera and makes it fresh and new. Be sure to read Abigail Nussbaum's review.

Incidents Around The House marks the evolution of Josh Malerman. Adept prose and commanding storytelling that effortlessly draws the reader in for a deeply unsettling and genuinely frightening read. A masterclass in scares. Highly recommended!

Still buzzing at how much I enjoyed 'Anyone But You'. An absolutely terrific romcom. Also shows how basically if you just cleave to the principles of a Nora Ephron romance you'll probably make a great romcom. And if you cleave to those principles while riffing off Shakespeare, you're cooking.

I am finally reading the Murderbot books after almost everyone I have ever met telling me to do so. And, one book down, they DO NOT disappoint. It reminds me of various 2000Ad strips, in a very good way.

I just finished Mariana Enriquez's Things We Lost in the Fire and damn! I don't know if I've ever read a short story collection with more affecting pacing. The stories are staggered so you never know how far the Horror will creep next time.

Book 1 of 2024 is Different Seasons by Stephen King 

I had never read any Stephen King before this and I was simply blown away. Four very different stories that affected me in so many different ways. Reading him is like when a scissors starts to glide through wrapping paper. Effortless.

Amal El-Mohtar is wonderfully generous to THE SAINT OF BRIGHT DOORS in the New York Times: “the best book I’ve read all year. Protean, singular, original … I can’t remember the last time a book made me so excited about its existence, its casual challenge to what a fantasy novel could be.”

but even they act in ways strange and broken

What is after capitalism

The paucity of our speculative fiction betrays that whatever it is, it is a future that capitalism itself cannot imagine. It is helping us to know that it is a system for children that we have grown past. Everything must die. It is preparing itself.

“We have deluded ourselves into believing the myth that capitalism grew & prospered out of the Protestant ethic of hard work & sacrifice. The fact is capitalism was built on the exploitation & suffering of black slaves & continues to thrive on the exploitation of the poor both black & white.”
-MLK

Marx affords the "economic" sphere of life priority because his ultimate concern is with human self-creation, and in class societies, self-creation is limited by how daily life is organized, which in capitalist society is in accordance with the law of value. Marx is not deterministic, capitalism is.

In the US, moving from the first quintile to the third or fourth in earnings is often enough to break the chain of generational poverty, but it's nowhere near enough to remove the scars of having been there.

Capitalism ruins everything because it requires taking and taking, constant growth and consumption of finite resources. 

Games becoming shitty and adding microtransactions? Social media platforms adding native ads and "pro" upgrades? Planes shrinking seats but charging more? 

THAT is capitalism.

A corporation is an entity controlled by shareholders that exploits its assets to create maximum benefit for its collective owners.
A union is an entity controlled by its members to exploit their assets (time, intellect, and labor) to create maximum benefit for its collective members.
Capitalism is alright at incentivizing people to bring new products to market but wow does its incentivization scheme fail once those products achieve any sort of scaled success.

Of course, the real tragedy of Moby Dick is that Ahab does not show enough consideration to his fiduciary responsibility to maximize shareholder value. In this TED talk I will show how the problems of capitalism presented by Melville can only be solved by more capitalism 🐋

I wish people truly felt the sheer obscene size of a billionaire's wealth.

Exhibit A)
One million seconds is about 11.5 days.
One billion seconds is about 31.5 YEARS.

Exhibit B)
If you get 100,000/yr &never spend a cent, you'd still have to work for 10,000yrs to become a billionaire.

No. Just no.

i'm absolutely not saying that you just gotta hand it to capitalism, but if you look at electric guitar design in the 1950s, it does look like an example of market competition spurring everyone to be better in a way that I feel like we get told always happens but don't actually always see

babe you’re not mad at “poptimism” you’re mad at music being a canary in the coalmine for 21st century market capitalism systematically turning everything that brings you pleasure into a bucket of live crabs

The utopian promise of socialism is that everyone’s labor will matter, and the hours of your life you give over to it will have a purpose besides “enhancing shareholder value” or meeting arbitrary quotas (tbh I think the collapse of the eastern bloc ultimately stems from their inability to do this)

its so fucked up how much 10 grand would change the average persons life, and how to a billionaire its literally a meaningless expense. if i earned ten thousand dollars a DAY every day, it would take me almost 68,500 YEARS!! to reach the estimated net worth of elon musk of 250 billion

Two-faced capitalism.

The clock at the top is for the bosses. It tells the actual time.

The clock at the bottom stopped or slowed when the factory machines stopped or slowed, so workers only got paid when the machines were running.

Bosses would still do this if they could.

I feel like the misunderstanding  of the differences between commerce vs capitalism and personal property vs private property is such a problem. It's sustainable to own what you make and sell the products of your labor, what causes problems is not that, but a different system based on exploitation.
Nothing says late-stage capitalism like closing a grocery store in one of the poorest neighborhoods in the county and replacing it with a plasma donation clinic.

The buried lede to “why don’t billionaires do [something fun and whimsical]” is that they are relatively cash-poor corporate entities wearing flesh suits. 

They have the money to live “well” but most of it is tied up in speculative assets which can’t be easily liquidated because they’re leveraged.
capitalism is the most efficient economic system ever produced except for healthcare and mcdonald's ice cream machines

By my calculation, as mostly a jaded Gen Xer, the only leverage we regular folks have is our labor. That’s it.
So what are we doing with it? And what are we doing to ensure those without that leverage (who can’t work) also have the freedom to live and thrive?
When you're immersed daily in rhetoric about the duplicity and depradations of capitalism, it becomes impossible to believe you've actually gotten a good deal at your weed store's Thanksgiving sale

so much of late stage capitalism is corporations making most of their money on tax, subsidy, writeoff, losses, etc. loopholes and very little on actually selling the product they make, which is why they don't give two shits about the product and layoff people by the hundreds every fucking quarter

Capitalism eventually produces monopolies due to capital seeking efficiency through economies of scale.
